data.title: Kura Oncology: Safety Profile Of Komzifti Supports My Upgrade To Strong Buy
data.description:
On December 4, 2025, I initiated coverage of Kura Oncology (KURA) in this article with a Speculative Buy rating.

The core of that thesis was straightforward. Kura is an early commercial stage biotech. In November 2025, the FDA approved its lead drug, Komzifti (ziftomenib), for relapsed or refractory NPM1-mutated acute myeloid leukemia [AML]. Under the current label, the realistic U.S. market opportunity is on the order of $350–$400 million annually.

The long-term value of Kura’s equity is not really about relapsed AML. It is about whether Komzifti can work in frontline AML, where the patient population is far larger and where patients often stay on therapy much longer. Frontline AML represents a three to six billion-dollar annual potential market in the United States alone. That is where enduring equity value would ultimately be created.

The reason Komzifti has a credible shot at that opportunity lies in its safety profile and biological positioning. Komzifti is a menin inhibitor. While not first in class, it has demonstrated a comparatively clean cardiac profile, particularly with respect to QT prolongation.

That detail is critical as modern AML treatment is built around drug combinations, and a menin inhibitor will not become part of frontline care unless it can be safely layered with venetoclax-based regimens, intensive chemotherapy, and FLT3 inhibitors. Komzifti’s profile materially improves its probability of being used in such combinations.

In my original article, I noted that the value of Kura as a company would ultimately be determined by the outcome of its frontline expansion strategy; therefore, an investment in Kura’s common stock is highly risky, although some risks are mitigated by an FDA-approved molecule and $501 million in cash held by the company as of Sept 30, 2025.

I noted the company has a profit-sharing arrangement with Kyowa Kirin, which any investor of Kura must take into account when projecting its future profits and stock valuation.

I also alluded in that article to the fact that CEO Troy Wilson purchased 100,000 shares at $7.13 per share in the open market in August and September 2025 at prices not far from where the stock trades today.

Those purchases were real cash purchases. In light of the subsequent volatility, the stock has effectively round-tripped back toward the levels at which Wilson himself was buying.

Stock Volatility and a Puzzling Price Decline

Since that article, Kura’s stock has declined from roughly $12 to around $8 today, dealing with its investors a short-term setback.

The selloff may have been driven by the company’s disclosure through this SEC Form 8-K filing of $2.1 million in net product revenue during the initial five-week commercial rollout of Komzifti, ended December 31, 2025. But that disclosure was made on January 11, 2026 — well after the stock had already fallen. The timing simply does not line up.

In fact, shortly before Christmas, Kura’s stock spiked more than 10% after Jim Cramer made the following comments on national television about Komzifti and Kura:

“I have always felt that if you have something that is for cancer and it works, then you have to own the stock as a speculation, but just as a speculation, because it’s been such a difficult disease.”

The subsequent slide back toward $8, therefore, remains mysterious to me. I welcome any comments below as to the causes of such price decline.

In any case, in small- and mid-cap biotech, prices often move hard without known triggers, driven as much by flows and mood as by any specific piece of news. Volatility is the core terrain that we may exploit.

What the December 8, 2025 ASH Presentation Actually Showed

Another potential cause of the stock price decline may be the company’s presentation at ASH on December 8, 2025, the slides of which can be found here.

During that event, the company presented Phase 1b data from KOMET-007 showing that ziftomenib combined with venetoclax and azacitidine produced:

• High remission rates in newly diagnosed NPM1-mutated AML (86% composite complete remission; 73% complete remission); • High rates of molecular MRD negativity; • Durable responses with a median duration of remission and overall survival not yet reached at the time of cutoff; and • A safety profile comparable to venetoclax/azacitidine alone, with only one interim QT-related event reported, which resolved, allowing the treatment to continue.

These data were used to justify advancement into the ongoing KOMET-017 randomized Phase 3 frontline study evaluating ziftomenib-based regimens in both intensive and non-intensive settings.

I do not see anything in this presentation that would naturally explain a material decline in the stock thereafter. If anything, the presentation confirmed the scientific case underlying Kura’s frontline clinical and consequently commercial potential.

The Reasons for My Upgrade

While the scientific foundation of the story has not been eroded, the valuation setup is different now. At today’s price, Kura’s market capitalization is approximately $750 million.

According to the same Form 8-K, the company held approximately $667 million in cash and equivalents as of December 31, 2025, including $195 million of milestone payments received in the fourth quarter of 2025 under its collaboration with Kyowa Kirin.

The company reports in its latest Form 10-Q approximately $468 million of liabilities, $317 million of which represented the contract liabilities under its partnership with Kyowa. These amounts reflect portions of the transaction price already received by the company that are allocated to future development and commercialization services the company is obligated to perform. They are not borrowings that must be repaid with cash.

Taken together, the market is currently valuing Kura’s operating business—including its FDA-approved product, commercial platform, and pipeline—at roughly $80 million (after accounting for cash), subject to $317 million in collaboration-related performance obligations plus $151 million in conventional debt obligations.

Third, the same Form 8-K reported $2.1 million of Komzifti net product revenue in the initial five-week commercial period. Even if one simply annualizes that early run rate—assuming no growth at all—Komzifti would generate approximately $21 million of annual net product revenue. That assumption is extremely conservative, as oncology launches are typically gradual and back-end loaded. That level of revenue shall support the market’s current valuation of the assets of the company subject to its indebtedness given the special nature of the company's debts, without assigning substantial value to the launch ramp of Komzifti or to the much larger frontline opportunity.

Fourth, the company also expects to recognize approximately $15–$17 million of collaboration revenue in Q4 2025. While non-cash, this further underscores the fact that collaboration structure may appear to create debt obligations upon the company; it also generates GAAP revenues and profits for the company.

What has changed is the stock price. At today’s level, investors are effectively paying substantially less for the assets, including the cash of the company and the frontline opportunity. Viewed in that light, the setup is materially more favorable than it was when I first wrote about Kura.

In short, the scientific and valuation foundations of my original thesis remain intact: Komzifti is an FDA-approved menin inhibitor with a safety profile that plausibly supports frontline combination use, and Kura has the financial resources and institutional knowledge to pursue that opportunity. For these reasons, I am upgrading Kura Oncology from Buy to Strong Buy.

data.title: This Isn't A Bubble At All - It's A Stock Picker's Paradise
data.description:
Introduction

I don’t think I’m breaking any news when I make the case that the market feels a bit uncomfortable right now. One reason for that is the market’s elevated valuation. That’s a topic we spent a lot of time discussing last year as well.

Usually, valuations are one of the biggest triggers for people to get either excited or fearful. After all, I think I can speak for everyone reading this when I say that we all try to buy assets below what we consider to be the fair value. If that weren’t the case, we wouldn’t buy them.

Moreover, many of the older investors among us have been through a number of cycles. I was just a kid in 2000, but I can imagine how buying at an elevated valuation when “everyone” said that the market would be fine has resulted in losses and years without real gains.

Using the data below, we can say that every dollar invested in the S&P 500 at the 2000 Dot-com peak needed roughly 14 years to break even on an inflation-adjusted basis.

So yeah, I fully get it when people are quick to become defensive whenever they see bubble indicators flash red. However, that’s also when big mistakes are made.

The tricky part is that, in hindsight, bubbles always look obvious, and everyone can monitor valuation indicators. That may drive sentiment, but I don’t think it does this market any justice.

That’s what this article is all about, as I will give you a market update, explain why this isn’t a bubble, and why I remain so excited to put capital to work.

So, let’s get to it!

Yes, Valuations Are an Issue

I may have a rather unusual thesis every now and then, but I’m not naive.

Valuations aren’t favorable.

While on a short-term basis, it’s fair to say that valuations don’t matter, data also support that on a long-term basis (10-year forward returns), valuations tend to matter a lot. Right now, the data tells us the market may show zero annual returns for at least a full decade.

That’s in theory. Also, note that this is based on just one data set, which is the market’s P/E ratio. However, this is getting support from other valuation metrics, including the Shiller CAPE. As Apollo reports (using the chart below):

The cyclically adjusted price earnings ratio, also known as the Shiller P/E, is a stock market valuation measure that divides the current stock price by the average of the last 10 years’ inflation-adjusted earnings, thereby smoothing out business cycle volatility to assess long-term over/undervaluation and predict future returns. It provides a better gauge of sustainable earnings power than the traditional P/E ratio, which uses only one year’s earnings. The latest reading shows that equity valuations are near the highest levels since 1880 [...]. - Apollo Global Management

In other words, so far, we have a flashing warning signal from the P/E ratio and one of the highest Shiller CAPE ratios since 1880. I can imagine, by now, you’re curious what I could possibly come up with to turn this into a bullish article.

It’s All About the Bigger Picture

One of the things we have discussed a number of times is that this market isn’t a “normal” market. Going into this year, the ten biggest stocks accounted for more than 40.0% of the entire S&P 500. That’s one of the highest numbers ever. As these companies account for 32.5% of the market’s earnings, we know that they have been a core driver of the market’s unfavorable valuation.

NVIDIA (NVDA) alone accounts for roughly 7% of the market.

Because any Mag-7 stocks were re-rated higher due to AI, the entire market benefited. To give you some proof, or at least some indication that supports my statement, I produced the chart below, which shows the ratio between the equal-weight S&P 500 (RSP) and the market-weighted S&P 500. The moment AI went mainstream, it caused the market to become more concentrated, causing the market-weighted S&P 500 (the “normal” S&P 500) to outperform.

That means at least two things:

  • This is likely a market of stocks instead of a stock market, as many companies may not have high valuations.
  • The market’s optimism that caused the re-rating of growth stocks doesn’t have to become bearish.

I believe the second bullet point is key, as AI isn’t a bearish factor. As I have written before, I believe we are now at a point where AI benefits are spreading from hyperscalers to the broader economy. Earnings may confirm that. Using the chart below, it’s time for two more bullet points:

  • Overall earnings growth is expected to remain strong. Combining both the Mag-7 and the rest of the S&P 500, we’re likely looking at prolonged growth, with each quarter of 2026 seeing double-digit EPS growth. When adding that AI could continue to enhance long-term margins, it supports the market’s valuation until the growth story starts to show cracks.
  • The S&P 493 (ignoring the Mag-7) is expected to see a growth acceleration. By the end of 2026, the rest of the market could show almost equal earnings growth to the S&P 500.

So far, this is bullish, with the biggest risk being that any growth headwinds could have an amplified impact on the market’s correction. I’m making that case because elevated valuations aren’t an issue as long as earnings growth is fine. In other words, while I expect the market to be fine, I also expect more volatility, as any negative headlines could create doubt and fears of overvaluation.

This is Basically About Two Markets

Essentially, we’re talking about two markets. One is the growth-focused market. The other market is the “real economy” market. Only one of these is associated with bubble talk right now.

One of the most important factors going forward is the business cycle. If we are in a late-style cycle, odds are even the “real economy” companies are on thin ice. After all, when economic growth goes south, there’s no point in trying to diversify beyond Big Tech. In such a scenario, the Mag-7 becomes attractive again, as these companies have terrific balance sheets and less cyclical risk.

The good news is that this isn’t a late-cycle economy.

As some readers may know, I have often talked about a cyclical recovery. In fact, while I expect “higher for longer” inflation, I see no stagflation. I have bet on the exact opposite happening, meaning both higher growth and higher inflation.

For now, that thesis is looking good and getting new confirmation.

Apollo Global Management even calls it an “Industrial Renaissance,” supported by a substantial boom in non-defense capital goods. Growth in that area has hit a new multi-year high after bottoming in 2023 and 2024. Both of these years saw a transportation recession, housing weakness, and other headwinds for a wide range of cyclical companies.

More evidence of this is the ongoing weakness in the leading ISM Manufacturing Index. As we can see below, if I’m right about this being a volatile bottoming process, we’re in the early stages of a cyclical expansion, not in the late cycle of a past boom. The evidence simply doesn’t support that.

In fact, I would even make the case that early-cycle environments tend to look exactly like this. In such environments, fundamentals start to turn, policy remains supportive, and investors hesitate because backward-looking indicators still look messy (sentiment).

Policy, for example, includes the president’s efforts to kickstart housing growth by proposing $200 billion in mortgage purchases to suppress interest rates. That’s both inflationary (it boosts liquidity and demand) and another piece of evidence that this administration is very serious about kickstarting economic growth.

One could even make the case that weakness in key areas like housing and manufacturing is bullish, as it triggers a response from policymakers and/or even the Fed. This also means many of these stocks are not overvalued yet, allowing us to buy quality at a value price.

That’s why I’m so excited.

We’re starting to see a confirmation in key indicators.

As we can see below, year-to-date, materials, industrials, and energy are all beating the S&P 500 by a wide margin. I expect this to continue, which is why I invested the majority of my new capital last year in the industrial and energy space.

Although I’m not throwing fresh capital at the rally after deploying more capital, I am rebuilding my cash position (by accumulating more new cash instead of selling other assets) to buy any correction that can help me add to cyclical stocks like Carlisle Companies (CSL), which is one of my favorite housing suppliers; Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL), which is my favorite trucking company; and players like TransDigm (TDG) that benefit from both cyclical and secular growth in aerospace. These are just a few ideas, but I think you now know where my head is at by now and what that could mean for you.

Hence, instead of trying to time the market in general, I have deployed more capital than ever over the past 14 months. All of these investments were made in companies with attractive valuations while the market traded at a lofty valuation. This excludes my investments made during the April/May sell-off last year due to the trade war.

Or, to put it differently, I am not chasing momentum or dismissing valuation risks but simply recognizing that this market isn’t homogeneous but a mix of super unique companies at the start of a potential cyclical recovery.

Call me crazy (I’m sure some of you will do that in the comments), but I like my odds despite the obvious headwinds.

Takeaway

This market looks very expensive. The good news is that this is mainly the case at the index level. This isn’t a homogeneous bubble, if you ask me. Valuations are massively distorted due to the market’s imbalance, which has created a lot of hidden opportunities in an economy that could see a cyclical upswing.

In tricky environments like this, being cautious is important, but it could also be a costly mistake. So, instead of avoiding equities, I think we should spend more time finding overlooked opportunities. Needless to say, I’ll help you with that and provide a lot more actionable ideas going forward.

Risks to My Thesis

The margin of error is slim at this point. My thesis relies on a cyclical recovery and strong S&P 500 earnings growth. Without it, it’s nearly impossible to defend the market’s valuation. That’s why even increasing expectations of a recession could trigger a steep correction and why I expect volatility to see a few spikes this year, as these valuations could amplify bad news.

data.title: Exelixis: High-Growth Oncology Name Trading At A Discount
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I believe that there is unfairly little attention focused on Exelixis, Inc. (EXEL) at Seeking Alpha, whereas in my opinion this stock has one of the highest growth potentials in the medium term. Exelixis can be described as a fast-growing business in the field of pharmacology, which manages to simultaneously maintain high-growth rates without sacrificing margins. In addition, the company supports its growth purely with its own money, without attracting debt funds and maintaining the debt burden at almost zero. The company's key products (Cabometyx) are confidently leading in their markets, and in addition to them, management is developing several more promising new drugs. The most interesting thing is that any company with such parameters would have long been trading at huge multiplier premiums to its peers. But not in the case of Exelixis—the stock price is currently adequate, and in some places there is even an underestimation. For all these characteristics, I like this company. I assign a "Strong Buy" rating to the company's shares with a target price of $55 and a growth potential of 25% in the baseline scenario over the year horizon.

Still A One-Product Story - But That’s Changing

I always try to tell you in detail about the company when I write about it for the first time. This is done primarily for readers unfamiliar with the company. In this chapter, I will try to briefly, in simple words, but in detail, describe how Exelixis operates.

As a pharmaceutical company, Exelixis is engaged in the development, manufacture, and sale of drugs for the treatment of cancer. The company has its own flagship product, and another promising drug is being prepared for market release. Almost all of the revenue is generated by sales of Cabometyx, a drug that helps cure certain types of cancer, in particular kidney cancer and rarer hormone-related tumors. This drug firmly occupies a leading position in its niche. It is noteworthy that last year it began to be used for the treatment of some other types of cancer, which allowed the drug to expand its presence in related niches. Outside the United States, the company provides a license to sell the drug to other companies, for which it receives its royalty income. The management, aware of all the risks of dependence on one drug, invests a lot of money in R&D in order to diversify its revenue sources. The main contender for this role and the most promising drug is zanzalintinib. It treats the most common types of bowel cancer, has recently successfully passed important trials, and is preparing to enter the U.S. market soon. There are four more promising drugs that, if the circumstances are right, can be marketed, but they are only at the development stage.

In order to accelerate the development of new drugs and increase the cost effectiveness of R&D, the management decided in August to appoint Dr. Dana T. Aftab as the new head. He has been working at the company since 1998 and has extensive experience in the development of pharmaceutical products. In August, the Board of Directors decided to slightly reorganize the business in order to better control costs. As a result of the decision, more than 130 people were laid off and the office in Pennsylvania was closed. Investors generally reacted positively to such changes. I believe that management is moving in the right direction. The strong results did not turn the management's head. On the wave of success, they could uncontrollably increase SG&A expenses, arguing for high business growth rates. As you can see, the opposite is true—management strictly controls costs in order to keep business margins high. In my opinion, this is commendable.

Q3 Outperformance Validates Business Momentum

Data for the fourth quarter and the whole of 2025 will be released in mid-February. I suggest analyzing the results for the third quarter in order to understand the current state of the company and formulate adequate expectations for the future report.

Revenue for Q3 increased by almost 11% YoY. The result is excellent and indicates a high demand for Cabometyx. In a press release, management emphasized that there was no price effect on revenue growth—the price of the drug remained virtually unchanged during the quarter.

The Cabometyx product itself continues to steadily gain market share in kidney cancer drugs. It is likely that as early as 2026, the market share will be above 50%, as the entire market is growing much slower than the sales growth of Cabometyx. This is due to the fact that other tumors are successfully treated with this drug, and it also combines well with other medications.

As a result, the growth rate of the Exelixis business significantly exceeds the growth rate of competitors from the sector. In fact, Exelixis is one of the best companies in terms of growth and business margins at the same time. This is rarely seen even in the most dynamically developing industries.

As can be seen from the table, the company demonstrates a very high level of profitability. At the same time, Exelixis directs most competitors to CapEx, which allows it to keep its free cash flow high.

The margin improvement trend continued in Q3. For the third quarter in a row, management has been diligently reducing SG&A costs. The same goes for R&D. Here, expenses decreased by 11% yoy against the background of the completion of expensive research phases. At the same time, it is important for a company to diversify its sources of income, so it will most likely have to increase its R&D expenses one way or another. Otherwise, you may lose out in competition if your closest competitors spend more money on development and eventually develop more effective drugs.

Net income increased by more than 60% in both GAAP and non-GAAP terms. EPS increased to $0.69 GAAP and $0.78 Non-GAAP, while in Q3’24 it was only $0.40 and $0.47, respectively. EPS growth was simultaneously impacted by revenue growth, cost cuts, and aggressive buybacks. The market had expected EPS of about $0.68–$0.69 and was pleasantly surprised by the strong outperformance of consensus forecasts. The consensus revenue forecast was also lower than the final result. Looking at so many strong results, management updated its forecasts for Q4 to improve its key financial indicators.

Undervalued Leader With Structural Advantages

In this chapter, I will talk about my DCF model, its prerequisites, and final evaluation. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2025-2030, discounting the forecast FCFs, and then calculating the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030) and also discounting. I add up the discounted FCF and terminal value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the market cap). With EV/EBITDA, this is the enterprise value from which net debt is also subtracted), which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

In my model, I assume that the revenue CAGR for 2025-2030 will be about 8-9%, compared to 21-22% for 2020-2024. This is due to the gradual decline in demand for the main product, the emergence of alternative drugs, and the consolidation of the market. After 2027, the share of Cabometyx will gradually decrease due to the introduction of new drugs. They will be the main drivers of growth. Most likely, Exelixis will begin actively acquiring new companies and raising debt in the next two to three years to support business growth and diversify its product line. Operating margins will continue to grow due to tight cost control; however, with the gradual decline in demand for Cabometyx, the need for R&D expenses will grow stronger and stronger. Therefore, the operating margin will grow slowly and will grow to 37-38% in the baseline scenario. The company spends little money on CapEx, compared to its peers. In my opinion, this trend will continue, and management will try to keep CapEx's share of revenue at 2-3%. As a result, the average FCF yield in 2025-2030 will be 27%, compared to 13% in the period 2020-2024.

I got the WACC for Exelixis at 7.04% (all prerequisites are listed in the table above).

The most surprising thing for me is the evaluation of the company. Exelixis is one of the best companies both in terms of business growth and profitability. It has the lowest debt burden among its competitors and conducts aggressive buybacks. With such parameters in the American stock market, stocks usually trade at huge premiums to the sector and their historical averages. But not in the case of Exelixis. For many multiples, we see large discounts of 20-30% compared to the sectoral averages. In my opinion, this is a clear underestimation of the company's shares.

The results of my DCF model fully confirm my thesis about undervaluation in stocks. In the base scenario, the share price should be in the range of $52-52, which is an upside of 18-25%. In more positive scenarios (the WACC is lower and the multipliers are higher), the growth potential is 32-38%. Interestingly, even in the most negative scenario, the stock price could rise by 6-13%. I recommend buying Exelixis shares with a target price of $55 per share and an upside of 25%.

Aggressive Buybacks Signal Confidence

There is practically no debt burden on the business. The total debt at the end of September was $177 million, with about $988 million in cash in the accounts, which results in negative net debt. Interest expenses are near zero and do not put pressure on net profit.

The fact that the company manages to maintain high business growth rates without attracting debt is a great advantage. Exelixis does not pay dividends, but aggressively buys shares from the market. In total, from 2023 to Q3 2025, the company repurchased almost $1.9 billion worth of its own shares at an average price of $26.84, greatly reducing the number of shares outstanding. In October 2025, management announced a new share repurchase program for up to $750 million by the end of 2026. At the current share price, this is equivalent to buying back 15-17 million shares or 5% of the total capitalization. Management is not going to review its strategy of paying profits to shareholders. It doesn't make much sense—everything is working well. But at some point, the company will have to raise debt in order to support growth. Most likely, Exelixis will want to enter the M&A market and make several strategic purchases to diversify its revenues. Then debt financing will be attracted. Dividends will begin to be paid in the distant future, when Exelixis will become a mature company.

Risks

The main risk is related to the complete dependence of the business on Cabometyx. The emergence of generics and alternative drugs from competitors can severely affect revenues from Cabometyx and, accordingly, the entire revenue of the business. At the same time, the success of new drugs is not guaranteed—this is the next risk. Zanzalintinib is a promising drug, but commercial success may be worse than expected. All other risks are related in one way or another to possible failures in development and regulatory changes in the United States, which may negatively affect the price of drugs.

Final Thoughts

In my opinion, this is one of the best investment ideas in the pharmaceutical market. The company's flagship drug is aggressively capturing market share, while Exelixis is preparing to bring a promising new drug to the market. The company itself is noticeably ahead of its competitors in the sector in terms of revenue growth and business profitability, managing to keep its debt burden at almost zero and aggressively buying shares from the market. I believe that those who are interested in shares of pharmaceutical companies should pay attention to Exelixis. My rating is Strong Buy.

data.title: Why CGGR Could Become A Quiet Winner In America's AI Revolution
data.description:
Introduction

I remember watching one of my favorite movies of all time, starring Will Smith and Shia LaBeouf, I, Robot, that debuted in 2004. In short, the film was set in the future and was about AI changing the way we live.

Robots were created to keep humans safe but eventually turned evil after being controlled by an artificial intelligence computer.

A particular scene in the movie that stood out to me was when Will Smith's character wanted to take a nap while driving and prompted AI to take over.

I thought to myself, could we ever see something like that in America? While I haven't heard or seen anything like that yet, companies like Tesla (TSLA) and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) have already introduced self-driving cars.

With my belief that America will continue to leverage artificial intelligence and that it will revolutionize the country in the coming years, focusing on AI stocks, or those with exposure, is paramount going forward.

And Capital Group Growth ETF (CGGR), despite its relatively short track record, looks poised to see solid growth as a result.

In this article, I discuss the fund's strategy, fundamentals, and why investors looking for growth should consider them for their portfolio.

How Will AI Impact America?

Although AI has been around for a while, in recent years the adoption has accelerated. The introduction of ChatGPT in late 2022 has been a game changer. And I believe going forward this will continue to be the case.

Businesses are seemingly leveraging AI to introduce synergies, improve efficiencies, and reduce waste. One way is by massive layoffs. In most businesses, your employees are usually your highest expense.

And we saw this with DOGE last year, expected to cut wasteful spending. DOGE headlines have seemingly dwindled, but AI will likely continue to change the way we do business.

Will there be more massive layoffs this year? Will unemployment continue to rise as a result? I, personally, believe that AI will continue to cause disruption in major industries this year that could impact unemployment further.

We're already seeing this in the case of office REITs (XLRE), which is why many trade at discounts in comparison to other REIT peers.

As you can see in the chart below, office REITs have significantly underperformed the largest ETF that tracks the S&P (SP500), Vanguard's (VOO), in the past year.

The market for 2025 closed up more than 16%. And I believe AI adoption will continue to be a market driver going forward. Below you can see the AI market is expected to grow rapidly by 2034, reaching close to $3.7 trillion.

As a result, Technology (XLK) stocks, or ETFs with high-tech exposure, will likely see strong growth as a result. Because I'm bullish and think AI will drive market returns, at least between 10% and 12% in 2026, here's why investors should consider this ETF.

Who is CGGR?

CGGR is a growth fund from the Capital Group family. The ETF has a relatively short track record with an inception date of February 22, 2022. So, less than 5 years since going public.

I, personally, like to see five years of performance. But more than a year is sufficient to get a picture of their performance. Since going public, CGGR has performed well, even beating the index in 2025.

CGGR closed up close to 20% compared to nearly 17% for VOO. This is due to their higher exposure to the Technology sector.

CGGR's current exposure is approximately 30%. Unlike a lot of funds, instead of powerhouse NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) being the top position, this is occupied by Meta Platforms (META).

Most of CGGR's holdings are in the U.S. at over 90%, with Canada the second largest. I particularly like that CGGR's tech exposure is reasonable at 30%.

While higher exposure could lead to higher growth, funds like the Roundhill Generative AI & Technology ETF (CHAT), which has more than 70% exposure to the sector, could be a higher risk.

For now, CHAT could be a higher reward play but would underperform immensely should the tech sector see a steep correction or prolonged pullback. Early 2000s, anyone?

CGGR is actively managed, something I also like in that this could lead to outperformance vs. passively managed funds. Although this is no guarantee, having a portfolio manager gives a fund more flexibility, in my opinion.

As a result, CGGR's expense ratio is higher but reasonable at 0.39%. I find actively managed ETFs with expense ratios below 1% to be acceptable. But 0.5% or below is even better.

The fund's investment objective is to invest in larger, faster-growing companies. And this is expected to provide strong capital appreciation as a result. CGGR also uses the S&P as its benchmark.

At the end of FY25, the tech sector accounted for 25.71% of the portfolio. And with AI likely to contribute to U.S. growth, I suspect this could continue to increase as a result.

CGGR currently has 102 holdings. So, their portfolio is more concentrated in comparison to any fund that tracks the S&P. This could also be the reason the fund has outperformed the index over the past 1 and 3 years.

And this has been led by their strong performance in NAV growth since inception, which outpaced the S&P by nearly 3%. Over the past 3 years, CGGR's NAV growth gap widened, up roughly 31% compared to 23% for the index.

Unique Strategy

Probably the best metric I like about Capital Group's ETFs is that it has multiple portfolio managers. This gives each their own section to manage, which gives their funds a higher chance of outperforming.

CGGR's sister fund, Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV), has 5 portfolio managers compared to 7 for the former. As previously mentioned, this, I believe, gives their funds a higher chance of outperforming as it alleviates the need of relying on one manager.

For comparison, CGGR has performed well, despite lagging behind powerhouses like Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) and Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) over the past 3 years. However, their performance was respectable, up 121%.

SCHG, a passively managed fund with a lower expense ratio, led the way, up over 136%. For transparency, SCHG is one of my favorite funds for growth exposure due to their low-cost structure and long-term performance. However, over the past year, CGGR outperformed SCHG and was slightly below QQQ.

While CGGR has lagged peers like SCHG and QQQ in the past 3 years, their performance vs. the S&P in the past 1 and 3 years shows their potential.

And I believe that they will likely continue to outperform the index going forward, providing investors with solid returns above the market average.

Cons

Although I've become a fan of Capital Group's ETF due to their unique strategy, CGGR does come with some cons as well.

  • Relatively short track record
  • Higher expense ratio vs. peers like SCHG & QQQ
  • Higher valuation compared to the overall index

Moreover, the Technology sector could suffer a steep crash or correction due to overvaluation. If so, the fund could see muted returns.

Final Thoughts

Despite their valuation, I rate CGGR a buy due to its Technology exposure and my belief that artificial intelligence will be a big driver in market returns going forward.

While a pullback could lead to lower growth vs. 2025, I believe we're still poised to see at least 10% to 12% returns minimum for the year.

As a result, I believe CGGR could deliver returns in a range of 11% - 14%, leading to another outperforming year for the fund.

Moreover, the fund's exposure to Communications (XLC) and Consumer Discretionary (XLY) provides diversification and potential downside protection should the market experience a prolonged pullback.

data.title: SEALSQ: A Hold Despite Post-Quantum Ambition
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

I do not think SEALSQ Corp. (NASDAQ:LAES) is an easy stock to underwrite, but it is also becoming increasingly hard to ignore.

The company works across secure silicon, device identity, and post-quantum cryptography, three areas that are quietly becoming non-optional across IoT, infrastructure, and industrial systems. Over the past year, it has also raised a significant amount of capital while expanding its technical footprint, and now, it looks like the company’s strategy is built around a post-quantum security roadmap.

To be clear, SEALSQ is not a mature security platform, and it is not a conventional semiconductor compounder either. We can see it with the fundamentals: small revenue and hugely negative margins, and much of its long-term narrative sits way, way ahead of the numbers. Even so, I think that the company’s setup going into 2026 looks more concrete than it did a year ago across its balance sheet, product development, and capital deployment, and I’m recommending a Hold on the stock.

What Does the Company Do?

I’d describe SEALSQ as a company that is trying to secure connected devices starting from the hardware layer, then extend that trust upward across the device lifecycle.

The core of the business today is still secure hardware. It designs and sells secure elements and secure microcontrollers that act as a hardware root of trust inside connected devices. These chips store cryptographic keys, handle authentication, and protect sensitive operations from tampering. This is where the bulk of current revenue comes from, and it is the entry point into most customer relationships.

On top of the silicon layer, we have provisioning and Root of Trust infrastructure. SEALSQ supports key injection and certificate provisioning at multiple stages, including during chip manufacturing, device assembly, and in-field deployment. This part of its business is important because large IoT and infrastructure deployments are more about how to manage identity and trust at scale.

The third layer is public key infrastructure (PKI) and trust services. This includes certificate issuance, lifecycle management, and authentication services that allow devices to be securely identified and managed over time. If you look at what the company has been up to recently, it’s clear that this is where it wants to move the business. In practice, this part of the model is still small in reported revenue, and I view it as an option on future mix shift, not a current earnings driver.

Beyond the core stack, SEALSQ is also expanding into custom ASIC and SoC design through IC’ALPS. It bought the French company last August, and the acquisition allows it to work with customers on tailored secure silicon rather than only standard products. It also pushes SEALSQ further upstream in the design process, and this can deepen customer relationships. At the same time, it brings some more complexity into the equation, especially around execution.

SEALSQ’s Growth Drivers, Positioning, and Market Opportunity

Across these markets, SEALSQ has relationships and design wins with Toshiba and Vodafone (smart metering), Legic (secure access systems), and other customers in the electronic lock manufacturing and medical industries in Japan, Taiwan, and the US. I’m reading this as a sign that the company is operating in real production environments, even though its revenue is still highly concentrated and customer order timing is another concern. Still, the takeaway from these opportunities is that SEALSQ is competing in environments where certification and compliance are part of the product.

Look at it this way: once any of the company’s secure elements or Root of Trust is certified and integrated into a customer’s design, it is very difficult for them to switch their supplier without going through a new requalification, regulatory review, and system redesign process. That creates a natural friction that benefits a business like SEALSQ, so it has the integration advantage.

For me, the clearest way to understand the company’s new positioning is to look at what it is trying to replace. It is trying to collapse that stack into a single security-first platform, starting at the hardware layer and extending through provisioning and trust services. Secure chips, key injection, certificate lifecycle management, and post-quantum readiness should work as one system rather than as loosely connected parts.

I think the post-quantum angle is a good example of where this distinction is even more crucial. Today, SEALSQ already supports post-quantum algorithms in its PKI services. On the hardware side, post-quantum secure microcontrollers are still largely on the roadmap, and the company expects to see some commercial impact a bit later than one would ideally like. That’s a gap between the hardware and the software side of things, and we will see more strides here over the next few years.

The most interesting part of how the company is planning for the future is its acquisitions, especially the planned Quobly one. Last week, SEALSQ announced that it has signed a non-binding MoU to make a minority investment into Quobly SAS, a French quantum computing company. Under the terms of that MoU, it could spend up to $200 million to acquire a majority stake of the company, albeit I’m not sure how much of the company they’re looking at buying out. Still, I take the announcement and potential move as a strategic signal. Quobly is a leader in its industry in France and Western Europe, so joining SEALSQ gives the latter an immediate complementary boost to its existing business and products. It also helps with its credibility.

Besides that, it means that we have to watch out for execution because investing alongside the quantum ecosystem requires you to have a plan for translating company insights and research into defensible, saleable products. Then, there’s the customization side of things. I mentioned its IC’ALPS acquisition last year, and that integration allows SEALSQ to engage its customers at the ASIC and SoC design level. That moves it upstream, where it can get the higher-value, longer-cycle projects.

Now, the market for these products and platforms is pretty large and growing quickly. TPMs (trusted platform modules) have an expected market value of nearly $5.5 billion in 2030, up from $3.3 billion in 2025. With post-quantum cryptography, the market size was $1.15 billion in 2024, and it has a CAGR of 37.6%, starting from 2025 and ending in 2030. For ASIC specifically, SEALSQ is also targeting a ¬$37 billion market. Those are just a few of the market opportunities.

These are eye-catching figures, and capturing even a tiny fraction of the markets should see SEALSQ become a company with annual revenues close to hundreds of millions by the end of those periods. But it’s not a straightforward trajectory, and I don’t want to equate the market size with inevitability.

For one, a $200 million pipeline isn’t a revenue backlog, but it’s a good starting point. At the same time, recording new design interest or wins doesn’t guarantee production revenue. I’m pointing this out because the investing market seems to have an idea around revenue figures and expectations for the next few years that is very different from the actual booked business the company has in the bag. In my view, the growth path that management seems to be going for rests on 3 key levers, which I’ll lay out below.

First, volume recovery in secure chips. The company has been managing a fairly weak period because of customer normalization and transition, but management expects baseline demand to recover. I think this is the least exciting driver, but it is the one that supports near-term revenue stability.

Second, there are new post-quantum product introductions, like chips that have FIP 140-3 and CC EAL5+ certification. We could start to see differentiation showing up materially here, but it is also where the execution risk is highest. Timelines matter, and customers will not redesign platforms lightly.

Finally, services attach over time. Provisioning and PKI services are supposed to ride along with hardware deployments. Today, we’re not seeing this as the case because they’re showing up only faintly in the numbers. I believe that long-term, it is the difference between a hardware supplier and a platform.

Overall, I think the outlook is very positive, and the question is whether SEALSQ can convert this early positioning into a sustainable revenue mix before dilution and competition start eating away the upside.

Where the Business Stands Today: The Numbers

SEALSQ’s financial profile only makes sense if you look at it across cycles, so here’s how I’d describe the last 2 fiscal years.

Period

Revenue ($m)

FY2023

~30

FY2024

~11

FY2025 (prelim)

~18

Q4 FY2025

~8

FY2024 was a reset year. Revenue fell to about $11 million, down sharply from FY2023, mostly because customers normalized their inventory and the company moved through a transition in its secure silicon roadmap. I’d say that the revenue decline exposed the operating leverage in the model and pushed the margins lower at exactly the wrong time.

Conversely, FY2025 was a rebound year. Going off what is in management’s preliminary results that came out a few days ago, we can see that revenue recovered to roughly $18 million, with a clear inflection in the second half of the year. Q4 alone contributed about $8 million, or close to 45% of full-year revenue, which tells me customer programs restarted and shipments resumed at scale. However, we must remember that this is still a sub-$20 million revenue business, and its revenue mix hasn’t changed much yet.

It’s the same picture with the gross margin. That was in the high-40s range in FY23, and it dropped to the mid-30s in FY24. It’s around the same range in FY25, albeit a tad higher as revenue rose, but still, growth without margin recovery does not fix the model. SEALSQ’s operating costs are even higher than ever because the components (SG&A, R&D, and sales) are built for a much larger business than it currently has, so it needs to scale revenue quickly.

With the balance sheet, it’s a nicer story. At the end of FY25, the company had around $425 million in cash, so it is exceptionally well capitalized for the medium term. That funding is mostly from equity offerings and warrant exercises, which is unsurprising, but it also changes the risk profile because liquidity isn’t a problem. The thing to consider is that it is shareholder dilution that is funding this growth, and we must consider whether the capital raised will produce enough incremental value per share to justify the company’s current valuation. That leads me nicely to discussing my position on the stock’s value.

I Believe That the Risk-Reward Favors a Hold

Today, SEALSQ’s stock is trading at $4.5 per share, and it has around 185 million shares outstanding. That gives it a market cap of $833 million. That valuation needs to be judged against where the business is today, even though the future looks promising.

The company exited FY25 with $18 million in revenue, and the valuation already embeds an incredibly generous forward view in my opinion. The company’s sales multiples are already at extremely elevated levels, and the only way they make sense is if it can record exponential sales growth while operating a more sustainable business model.

Now, let us look at the guidance for FY26. Management is guiding 50-100% revenue growth for the year, and that increase will largely come from the 3 levers I laid out in the previous section. That gives us a reasonable framework for expectations. Using FY25’s numbers as the anchor, SEALSQ should make between $27 and $36 million in revenue in FY26.

At today’s equity value of $833 million, the implied forward multiples look like this:

Scenario

FY2026 Revenue

Implied P/S

Low case

~$27m

~31x

Mid-point

~$31.5m

~26.4x

High case

~$36m

~23x

Those are not conservative multiples—they’re 8 to 10 times higher than the sector median of 3.5x. I don’t think they’re justifiable, especially for a business that is still reliant on hardware for sales while losing a ton of money and trying to transition into services as the main revenue driver. Simply put, even if we assume the absolute highest case within reason, so up to $40 million in FY26, the multiple still comes out to >20x. SEALSQ is still priced like it is a premium growth platform, and it is not that yet. There’s very little margin for error here.

If we also assume that it will recognize the rest of the pipeline in revenue (¬$160 million) across 2027 and 2028, it is still likely that there will be bigger share dilutions to fund that growth too, so it's not a straightforward ramp-up in share quality.

I can’t deny that the business has real momentum and improving fundamentals, but the fact is that we’re still waiting to see its products and trust services translate into revenue and materially improve gross margin. The latter, especially, is an outcome that is not fully certain heading into FY26, and I’m unwilling to pay such a premium for the stock at this time.

Key Risks to Look Out for and the Near-Term Catalysts

I’ve discussed several of the risks around SEALSQ’s business at several points during this article, so I'll just list a few below.

  • Revenue quality risk

  • Margin risk

  • Dilution and capital allocation

That last point is especially interesting because even though the company won’t struggle for cash in the medium term, I think it is likely that management will come back to the market for more funding at some point in the next two years. Why? Well, there’s the potential investment in and acquisition of Quobly ($200 million, and then some), and I believe that it is reasonable to assume that they will continue to pursue expansion and strategic investments in that timeframe. $400 million just won’t be enough.

Now, let me outline what I’m watching as catalysts moving forward. The most important is evidence that the second-half FY2025 inflection carries into FY2026. I think I speak for a section of the market when I say that in SEALSQ’s case, quarterly revenue consistency will start to matter more than headline annual growth over the next few years. A few strong quarters in a row will go a long way toward validating management’s outlook.

Another one is clearer disclosure around services and recurring components. I’d like to see some growth, however modest, in its deferred revenue or contract liabilities section on the balance sheet. In my opinion, that would signal that the business model is evolving in the right direction.

It’s a Hold for Me

I firmly believe that SEALSQ is operating in the right markets and with the right long-term questions in mind. The pieces are in place for it to grow quickly, and I’m watching how it executes. However, I’m not convinced that the current valuation represents a good entry point or has meaningful upside for now because it is far ahead of the company’s near-term trajectory.

I’d like to see broader revenue growth and recovering margins before getting more constructive again. If those pieces fall into place and the company outperforms the expectations it has set for itself, then I’ll be more inclined to consider changing my position. For now, it is a Hold for me.

data.title: BPRE: Interesting CRE CEF After The Conversion
data.description:
Thesis

Finance folks seem to love their rocks—after BlackRock and Blackstone, we are now going to examine a fund from Bluerock, an institutional alternative asset manager based in New York.

As per their webpage, Bluerock:

Is a leading institutional alternative asset manager based in New York with regional offices across the U.S. Bluerock has more than $19.5 billion in acquired and managed assets and offers a complementary suite of public and private investment programs, with both short and long-term goals, to individual investors seeking solutions aimed at providing predictable income, capital growth, and tax benefits.

In today's article, we are going to focus on the Bluerock Private Real Estate Fund (BPRE), a fund of funds in the CRE space. BPRE has been recently listed on the New York Stock Exchange as a CEF, thus now falls in the respective market segment.

What is the origin of this fund?

BPRE is interesting because it used to be an interval fund until late 2025, when it got listed:

New York, NY (September 25, 2025) – Bluerock is pleased to announce shareholder approval of a key proposal in connection with the listing of the Bluerock Total Income+ Real Estate Fund (“TI+” or the “Fund”) on the New York Stock Exchange. The proposal¹, recommended by independent proxy advisory firms Institutional Shareholder Services Inc. and Glass Lewis & Co. and the Fund’s Board of Trustees, was overwhelmingly approved by shareholders at the Fund’s special meeting held on September 25th, with over 81% of votes cast in favor of the initiative. In an earlier special meeting held on September 3rd, shareholders approved 13 additional proposals related to the listing.

Remember that interval funds are vehicles that are not listed; liquidity is not readily available to purchase or sell shares:

An interval fund is a type of investment company that periodically offers to repurchase its shares from shareholders. Their shares typically do not trade on the secondary market. Instead, their shares are subject to periodic repurchase offers by the fund at a price based on net asset value. They are permitted to (and many interval funds do) continuously offer their shares at a price based on the fund’s net asset value.

Due to this structure, many investors are locked in for long periods of time and thus do not benefit from the liquidity offered by listed vehicles. Many interval funds structure themselves this way because they purchase illiquid assets and thus need to structure their cash holdings for redemptions accordingly.

Once an interval fund gets listed, the market assigns a 'mark-to-market' to the underlying holdings, thus discounts to NAV appear. The CEF now trades with a -35% discount after its listing in December 2025.

What does the fund hold?

Unlike many CRE funds we reviewed, BPRE is a 'fund of funds', meaning it does not hold CRE loans or buildings outright but holds shares in other private CRE funds:

If we look at the holdings list, we can see the name holds shares in other private CRE funds, such as the CBRE Logistics Partners, the Carlyle Property Investors fund, or the Bain Capital Real Estate Fund I. These are not vehicles that can be accessed by retail investors because they are private and have very high minimum investment amounts. Thus, holding BPRE gives a retail investor access to the underlying investment vehicles that would otherwise be unreachable.

BPRE's holdings are diversified when it comes to the targeted segments:

  • Industrial funds: 28.3%.
  • Multisector funds: 20.5%.
  • Specialty: 14%.
  • Residential: 8.4%.
  • RE debt: 19.3%.
  • Cash: 9.6%.

The holdings list provided by the fund does contain some details regarding the funds (such as start date, average LTVs, etc.), but it is by no means exhaustive or very transparent. Unlike outright CRE REITs, where an investor has transparency into every loan, one buys into a portfolio of other funds here.

Does a large discount to NAV mean a massive upside?

The quick answer is no. The reality is more nuanced, however. We would split the large discount into two segments:

  • Actual write-downs are priced by the market.
  • Discounts that will close eventually.

The issue with many private interval funds and related accounting is that they do not need to take write-downs until loans mature, and they cannot be restructured anymore. In other words, the accounting is very permissive in terms of kicking the can down the road. This simply means the fund does its accounting at full notional value, when in reality, a true mark to market would be much lower. But this is a book value accounting versus a mark-to-market one.

When you allow a vehicle to be priced on an exchange, the market will do the pricing for you and assign your holdings a certain value. Right now, the market is telling us there are write-downs to be taken in the holdings (by each underlying fund), and there is also a discount to account for the uncertainty associated with other holdings.

This is not unusual. Keep in mind the trading levels for CRE REITs:

Ares Commercial Real Estate Corporation (ACRE) trades with a 49% discount to book, while KKR Real Estate Finance Trust Inc. (KREF) trades with a 42% discount. We wrote research articles for these funds and articulated why there are substantial write-downs to be taken from a fundamental perspective, with the market pricing that in.

So what does BPRE offer over CRE REITs?

BPRE is a bit of a different animal when compared to a CRE REIT. The CEF does not own CRE loans or buildings but shares in a very diverse portfolio of private CRE funds. The name is thus very, very diversified when it comes to managers and geographic reach. We like this aspect.

On the other hand, an investor here has very little transparency in the actual risks, but it is more of a faith-based diversified approach to CRE investing.

The fund currently comes with the following analytics:

  • AUM: $3.47 billion.
  • Yield: 8.5%.
  • Payment period: monthly.
  • Management fee: 1.5%.

The name recently transitioned to monthly payment dates in order to make itself more appealing to investors.

We find BPRE to be an interesting diversification proposal in the space, with the caveats around a lack of transparency. Given the recent listing, it is hard to tell how much of the discount represents what we would call 'repressed liquidity', i.e., investors who wanted to get out before but could not. We will follow BPRE going forward and are a 'Hold' for now.

Conclusion

BPRE is a CRE closed-end fund. The name is a new addition to the liquid space, having been an interval fund until December 2025. BPRE is a fund of funds, rather than holding CRE loans outright. The name provides access to a portfolio of private CRE funds from very reputable managers. Despite its large discount to NAV, the market is signaling write-downs that have not been taken yet by the underlying holdings, very similar to the listed CRE REIT space. We are watching this name closely, but we are on hold for now.

data.title: Thornburg Strategic Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Despite volatility, our focus continues to be on generating attractive income and risk-adjusted total returns over the long term.

Portfolio Managers

Lon Erickson, CFA

Portfolio Manager

Ali Hassan, CFA, FRM

Portfolio Manager

Christian Hoffmann, CFA

Head of Fixed Income

Supported by the entire Thornburg investment team

Market Review

U.S. fixed income markets concluded 2025 on a positive note, with broad gains driven by Federal Reserve rate cuts, cooling inflation, and broadly resilient economic data despite challenges like the U.S. government shutdown and labor market softness. The Federal Reserve continued its easing cycle with 50 basis points of rate cuts, including a widely expected 25bp cut at the December FOMC meeting that lowered the Fed Funds rate to 3.50-3.75% alongside a $40B/month Treasury bill purchase program, though the FOMC minutes reflected continued divisions within the committee. November saw significant volatility in rate expectations with December futures probabilities dropping below 30% at one point following hawkish signals from Chair Powell and other Fed officials. Inflation showed continued disinflation with November CPI coming in below consensus at 2.7% year-over-year (core 2.6%), while mortgage rates dropped for three straight weeks to end 2025 at their lowest point for the year. The government shutdown delayed most employment and inflation data in October with impacts continuing into November, creating what analysts called a "data vacuum."

Treasury yields fluctuated throughout the period, with short-end yields declining approximately 20-30 basis points as rate-cut expectations increased. The curve continued to steepen with the 30-year yield rising as short-end rates fell. The job market in late 2025 was characterized by labor market softening, with unemployment reaching a four-year high of 4.6% in November amid layoff announcements from major companies including Meta, Target, Amazon, Molson Coors, and Applied Materials.

Fourth Quarter 2025 Performance Highlights

In 4Q25 the portfolio (I share class) (TSIIX) returned 1.19%, -1 basis point behind the Bloomberg U.S. Universal Index. Year to date, the portfolio (I share class) returned 7.56%, -2 basis points behind the index.

Sector allocation and security selection contributed positively to relative performance with the portfolio’s CMO and corporate allocations providing uplift of 5 basis points each. The portfolio’s overweights to BB and Not Rated issues resulted in a net positive contribution of 15 basis points to relative performance as credit spreads modestly narrowed.

The portfolio maintained an average duration of 4.17 versus 5.79 for the index. Duration and yield curve positioning contributed a combined 15 basis points to relative performance for the quarter. The yield curve continued to steepen across the quarter with short-end rates falling and long-end rates rising driven by a combination of Fed cuts and favorable economic data.

Current Positioning and Outlook

The portfolio remained positioned with a longer duration and higher quality bias compared to its long-term range, driven by attractive real yields and tight credit spreads. Although we maintain a generally cautious view of the data center and AI sectors, many hyperscaler firms demonstrate strong credit profiles and will require ongoing investment to expand their AI infrastructure. In recent months, we have identified attractive opportunities within project finance, specifically through lending secured by data centers under construction with leases to investment-grade tenants. These transactions are not intended as broad sector calls but rather as select opportunities characterized by high barriers to entry, which can deliver favorable valuations, robust structures, and desirable tenant profiles.

The global economy has shown surprising resilience despite mounting pressures from the delayed impact of tariffs and constrained fiscal flexibility. With ongoing uncertainty, careful portfolio construction remains essential. Instead of crowding into popular trades or implementing static allocations, we urge investors to focus on strategies that balance liquidity, potential returns, and diversification, while staying flexible to adapt to new opportunities. In 2026, those who favor high-quality fixed income, selectively add real assets for stability, and seek undervalued equity sectors may benefit most.

We maintained broad diversification across fixed-income sectors with an emphasis on sectors demonstrating resilience or offering compelling risk/reward profiles identified through our research. Credit spreads remain tight, particularly within private market areas that have experienced sharp growth. Despite a generally strong background for equities, shares of several large alternatives firms have seen declines. More broadly, recent bankruptcies and instances of fraud may be indicative of light covenants typically seen late-cycle. Investors should consider if their holdings justify the risks of credit, illiquidity, falling yields, and limited transparency.

Basis Point (bp) – A unit equal to 1/100th of 1%. 1% = 100 basis points (bps).

U.S. Treasury securities, such as bills, notes and bonds, are negotiable debt obligations of the U.S. government. These debt obligations are backed by the "full faith and credit" of the government and issued at various schedules and maturities. Income from Treasury securities is exempt from state and local, but not federal, taxes.

A bond credit rating assesses the financial ability of a debt issuer to make timely payments of principal and interest. Ratings of AAA (the highest), AA, A, and BBB are investment-grade quality. Ratings of BB, B, CCC, CC, C and D (the lowest) are considered below investment grade, speculative grade, or junk bonds.

Credit Spread/Quality Spread - The difference between the yields of securities with different credit qualities.

Duration - A bond's sensitivity to interest rates. Bonds with longer durations experience greater price volatility than bonds with shorter durations.

Yield Curve - A line that plots the interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality, but differing maturity dates.

Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) - The branch of the Federal Reserve Board that determines the direction of monetary policy. The FOMC is composed of the board of governors, which has seven members, and five reserve bank presidents. The president of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York serves continuously, while the presidents of the other reserve banks rotate their service of one-year terms.

Fed Funds Rate - The interest rate at which a depository institution lends immediately available funds (balances at the Federal Reserve) to another depository institution overnight.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligation (CMO) - A type of mortgage-backed security that creates separate pools of pass-through rates for different classes of bondholders with varying maturities, called tranches. The repayments from the pool of pass-through securities are used to retire the bonds in the order specified by the bonds' prospectus.

Mortgage Pass-Through - A security consisting of a pool of residential mortgage loans. Payments of principal, interest and prepayments are "passed through" to investors each month.

data.title: Macquarie Corporate Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index credit spreads widened 4 basis points (bps) over the quarter, underperforming duration-matched Treasurys by 5 basis points but still generating a +0.84% total return. Shorter-duration, wider-trading sectors such as autos and office real estate investment trusts (REITs) led performance. Airlines also outperformed amid strong results from United Airlines (not held in the Fund) and an upgrade by Moody’s to Baa3.

The cable and media sectors led underperformers over the quarter as they remained under pressure from secular weakness and mergers and acquisitions. The technology sector also lagged amid heavy supply to fund data center build outs and growing capital expenditure (capex) needs. Investment-grade supply for the quarter totaled $311 billion (source: Bank of America), the strongest fourth quarter supply in history as hyperscaler and M&A issuance contributed to the record total. This included an $11 billion deal from Verizon Communications, Inc. (VZ) to fund the acquisition of Frontier Communications, Inc. (FYBR) and artificial intelligence (AI)-related deals from Alphabet, Inc. (GOOGL) ($17.5 billion), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) ($15 billion), and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) ($30 billion). Technicals remain strong and demand for investment-grade credit continues to be robust with more than $20 billion of inflows to investment-grade funds for the quarter and $69 billion for the full year (source: Lipper).

Source: Bloomberg, unless otherwise noted.

Within the Fund

For 4Q, Nomura Corporate Bond Fund Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund’s benchmark, the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index.

Key contributors to performance:

  • Non-Cyclicals – Security selection contributed to performance, driven by an underweight to longer-dated, higher-quality sectors such as pharmaceuticals and healthcare which lagged amid a steepening Treasury curve and improved risk sentiment.
  • Banking – Security selection generated positive performance, driven by an overweight to hybrid debt as these shorter-duration (to call) and higher-beta issues outperformed over the period.
  • High Yield – Exposure to this out-of-benchmark asset class generated positive performance as high yield outperformed investment grade given its shorter-duration profile and positive risk technicals.

Key detractors from performance:

  • Electric – Security selection generated negative performance, driven by an overweight to long-end spread duration within utilities as longer-dated issues lagged amid the rise in long-end rates.
  • Energy – Similarly within the independent exploration and production (E&P) sector, an overweight to longer-duration holdings was a drag on performance amid rising rates over the period.
  • Futures – Net long Treasury futures positions to hedge curve exposure detracted from performance as rates rose over the last two months of the quarter.

Outlook

We believe the strong technical backdrop, stable credit fundamentals, and a relatively benign interest rate environment will remain supportive for credit spreads in 2026 with a somewhat stable macroeconomic backdrop as many of the themes from 2025 (rate volatility, tariff impacts, and so forth) carry over into 2026. Risks will be more idiosyncratic in nature and skewed towards individual credits and sectors in the form of M&A, leveraged buyouts, and a significant increase in AI capex. We believe these factors will create dislocations in the market and will provide opportunities for investors. As a result, we expect spreads to trade in a narrow range of 75 bps to 95 bps for 2026 as investors will demand additional premiums to absorb the uptick in gross and net issuance, with relative value relationships getting repriced across sectors and ratings. We favor BBB-rated credits as earnings have been relatively strong and technicals are favorable with the BBB index share now at a decade low (source: Barclays). A key risk to our view is growth significantly slowing down as higher inflation further pressures consumers with tighter lending conditions.

Gross corporate bond issuance is expected to reach an all-time high of $1.8 trillion (source: JP Morgan), driven by three main factors: a wave of refinancing as more than $1 trillion of maturities come due; an uptick in M&A transactions; and a dramatic increase in debt-funded capex from the technology sector. The AI investment boom is front and center, with hyperscalers (Alphabet, Amazon, Microsoft Corp., Oracle Corp., and Meta) projected to account for more than $300 billion in new supply (source: JP Morgan) as data center construction and infrastructure accelerates. Net supply (gross supply less redemption activity including maturities, calls, tenders, and coupon income) is also expected to increase in 2026 at roughly $302 billion (source: JP Morgan) but still remaining manageable and well below the peak experienced during the pandemic. Given that spreads are expected to remain at historically tight levels, the sheer volume of supply, especially from technology and media, will be a key driver of sector performance, highlighting the importance of sector selection. Financials and healthcare should outperform based on strong fundamentals, manageable event risk, and lower supply pressure, while technology, media, and retail are likely to lag as elevated issuance and leverage weigh on valuations. Non-cyclicals should outperform cyclicals, reflecting a more cautious consumer outlook and subdued retail demand. We expect that finance companies, mainly business development companies (BDCs), will continue to be volatile with fears growing around the lack of transparency into the private credit assets held by BDCs. This could also impact life insurers given the rising use of private credit in general account portfolios. We believe such volatility will create investment opportunities in the sector. Finally, demand from institutional and foreign investors should remain robust based on historically attractive all-in yields and with investor positioning remaining manageable.
